title: Addresses and dereference

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The address of a live int is passed to a helper, checked against null by contract, and dereferenced only before that int's lifetime ends. Your goal is to trace pointer and pointee state and identify null, invalid and dangling cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Parameters and return values. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A pointer value designates an object or function, one-past position, or null; dereference is valid only when the designation, lifetime, alignment, and access type permit it. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Uninitialized, dangling, misaligned, out-of-bounds, incorrectly converted, or null pointers cannot be made safe merely by checking their numeric appearance. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Any non-null pointer is safe to dereference because null is the only invalid address value.

includes: Create, inspect and dereference pointers only while they designate valid objects.
Trace pointer and pointee state and identify null, invalid and dangling cases.
excludes: Uninitialized, dangling, misaligned, out-of-bounds, incorrectly converted, or null pointers cannot be made safe merely by checking their numeric appearance.

outcomes: Trace pointer and pointee state and identify null, invalid and dangling cases.

examples.title: Addresses and dereference: defined case
examples.situation: The address of a live int is passed to a helper, checked against null by contract, and dereferenced only before that int's lifetime ends.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: The address of a live int is passed to a helper, checked against null by contract, and dereferenced only before that int's lifetime ends.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Dereference requires a pointer to a live compatible object within bounds; non-null alone does not establish provenance, lifetime, alignment, or access validity.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Dereference requires a pointer to a live compatible object within bounds; non-null alone does not establish provenance, lifetime, alignment, or access validity.

examples.title: Addresses and dereference: boundary case
examples.situation: Returning the address of an automatic local creates a dangling pointer once the function returns and its object lifetime ends.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Returning the address of an automatic local creates a dangling pointer once the function returns and its object lifetime ends.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Uninitialized, dangling, misaligned, out-of-bounds, incorrectly converted, or null pointers cannot be made safe merely by checking their numeric appearance.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Any non-null pointer is safe to dereference because null is the only invalid address value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace pointer and pointee state and identify null, invalid and dangling cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any non-null pointer is safe to dereference because null is the only invalid address value.
misconceptions.correction: Dereference requires a pointer to a live compatible object within bounds; non-null alone does not establish provenance, lifetime, alignment, or access validity.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Any non-null pointer is safe to dereference because null is the only invalid address value.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Addresses and dereference: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Uninitialized, dangling, misaligned, out-of-bounds, incorrectly converted, or null pointers cannot be made safe merely by checking their numeric appearance.
